formatted_address: 122 NW 13th Ave, Cape Coral, FL 33993, USA
pitch_rationale: The subject home (center, covered in blue emergency tarps) is located in Cape Coral, FL — a region where 4:12 is the dominant residential pitch due to hurricane building codes and climate norms. The blue tarps lie very flat against the roof planes with minimal draping, indicating a shallow slope. Ground shadows cast from the eave edges are short relative to the building's plan footprint, consistent with a low rise:run ratio. The hip roof geometry shows a wide eave perimeter with the ridge sitting close to the center, and no dormers or steep gable protrusions are visible. The neighboring gray-shingle hip roof to the south similarly shows minimal vertical prominence, corroborating the shallow regional pitch norm. All cues point to a 4:12 pitch, typical for SW Florida construction.
footprint_rationale: The subject roof is fully covered with blue tarps, which clearly delineate the roof boundary. The orange bounding box measures approximately 260 px × 245 px (56.9 ft × 53.6 ft). The roof is L-shaped with a main section (~42.7 ft × 25.2 ft) in the upper-left and an attached wing/garage (~51.4 ft × 28.4 ft) in the lower-right, with a rectangular notch (~14.2 ft × 25.2 ft) cut from the upper-right corner. L-shape fill factor ~75% of the 3,050 sq ft bounding rectangle yields ~2,290 sq ft, confirmed by direct polygon summation at ~2,330 sq ft; settled on 2,300 sq ft. The roof is a fully-hipped style (no visible gable ends or rake edges). Two interior valley lines form at the L-junction where the two hip sections meet. Ridge segments estimated from visible tarp ridge lines. Eave perimeter traced around the full outer L-shape boundary. Confidence is moderate-low (0.42) due to the tarp obscuring fine structural details and the complex L-shaped geometry making precise corner locations difficult to pin down.

findings.description: Bright blue tarps cover the entire main roof surface — both the primary hip roof section and the lower secondary roof section (likely garage or addition). The tarps are large, uniform blue in color, and span all visible slopes with no underlying shingle material visible beneath them.
findings.location_description: Whole roof — all slopes of both the main structure and the lower attached section

findings.description: A lighter blue-white section of tarp on the lower-front (south-facing) slope is visually distinct from the darker blue tarps on the upper sections, indicating a separate, overlapping tarp layer or partial underlayment exposure.
findings.location_description: South slope of the lower attached roof section, center to right half

condition.rationale: The subject roof at 122 NW 13th Ave, Cape Coral, FL 33993 is almost entirely covered by blue tarps across all visible roof slopes — both the main hip roof and the lower attached section (likely a garage or addition). Blue tarping of this scale and completeness is a strong indicator of significant prior storm damage (consistent with hurricane activity in the Cape Coral/Lee County area) or an active roof replacement in progress. No underlying shingle material is visible on any slope. A second, lighter-colored tarp layer is visually distinct on the south slope of the lower section, suggesting layered emergency coverings. This roof warrants immediate in-person inspection to assess the extent of underlying deck or structural damage before any replacement quote can be finalized.
